dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 78 responding for unconscious male in driveway.
Hi, I'm Tony, a paramedic with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
He fainted and didn't wake up right away. I tried to nudge him, but he was unresponsive.
I called 911 right away. He is 73.
Can you see me, sir?
He's sluggish but responded to touch.
He wasn't opening his eyes initially.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me where we are today?
I think [COMPANY_NAME]? Or maybe I'm still at my house?
You're at your house right now. What day is it?
Tuesday... I think it might be June 12th...
Mr. Smith, can you squeeze my fingers for me? Good, that's a solid response. You're following commands.
Looks like he has good motor response abilities.
Sir, let me check your airway. Can I do that?
Okay.
You're breathing steadily and airway is patent and unobstructed.
Respirations are 12, regular.
Oxygen saturation is 100 just now but has dropped since.
Pulse is regular at 64 and strong.
Skin is diaphoretic, moist, and clammy now but has improved.
Blood pressure is 155 over 76 initially but is currently 128 over 66.
Have you had symptoms like this before, sir?
Yes, about two weeks ago - a hypoglycemic episode with the same presentation.
What medications is he currently on?
He was told he was diabetic, but medications are not documented on him today.
Does he have any known allergies?
None that I'm aware of.
All of his medical history was relayed to us by his wife. He is a known diabetic, with bilateral lower leg amputations, both above the knee.
He mentioned he was sitting in his wheelchair before the event started.
I was sitting outside in my wheelchair when I suddenly felt very sluggish, like I couldn't control myself anymore.
He fell out of his wheelchair, as per his wife who witnessed the event.
He's 73 years old, weighs about 130 pounds. His full name is George Oscar Smith.
Thank you, Mrs. Smith. When was his last food or fluid intake, sir?
I had something to eat this morning, but I can't remember exactly when or what it was.
His GCS is 15 - eye 4, verbal 5, motor 6, so he's alert and oriented.
Mr. Smith, do you have any pain currently?
No, no pain at all.
His skin is moist, diaphoretic, and shows mild pallor at times but is improving since treatment began.
Mr. Smith, how does your skin feel - warm, cool, or normal to you today?
It's a little warmer than normal but better now than it was a few minutes ago.
Any headache or neck pain at all, Mr. Smith?
No pain in the head or neck at all, just in my arms I guess when I hit the ground.
Do you have a history of heart attack or stroke like this before, sir?
No, but he's got a bilateral lower leg amputation - both above the knee, and he's been a wheelchair user for a while.
Starting ECG now. Four-lead monitoring initiated as per diabetic hypoglycemia protocol 3-8.
Pulse is 64 again at this time.
Respirations remain 12, regular and unlabored.
Blood pressure is 128 over 66 at this time as well.
Oxygen saturation is at 89 percent currently but was 100 earlier when first assessed.
EKG shows sinus rhythm and no acute cardiac changes from the start of our care, as per ECG trends so far.
Baseline blood glucose was 36 and continuing to rise appropriately after treatment was applied today, now at 86 during transport preparations.
Sir, we're going to give you some oral glucose now to raise your blood sugar level. This is part of our standard hypoglycemia treatment protocol 3-8 as well.
Oral glucose, 15 grams, administered buccal, and patient was able to take the dose without any resistance or difficulty, as documented in the report so far.
We then started an IV and began administering Dextrose 10% solution, 25 grams intravenously, as part of our hypoglycemia treatment.

The patient had a prior episode two weeks ago. Today's treatment plan began with OBG glucone before initiating D10% intravenous solution since the patient is non-ambulatory and requires careful handling due to his amputation and medical background.
18G IV placed in the left arm for D10% administration as another step in hypoglycemia intervention under protocol 3-8 today, as stated in field impressions and narratives.
Blood sugar improved: first 36 baseline, then to 45 post-glucose, and now up to 86 just before our departure time today after all interventions.
Blood pressure is 128 over 66, pulse 64, and respiratory rate 12, consistent with trended data just now.

Oxygen saturation is 89 percent still and we observed an initial rapid drop from 100 percent, but that trended in line with expected bgl changes during thermal shift and activation phase of hypoglycemia.

IV is patent now and patient hasn't reported any burning sensation or complaint at the site thus far, as also verified by note from on-scene personnel and by myself as lead partner currently managing the iv assigned to left arm and also documented during protocol steps to date.
Mr. Smith, how are you feeling now? Any alertness or confusion still present today?
Much better now. I feel more aware of what's going around me right now, so I think I am doing well.
You were sluggish earlier, but now your mind is sharper and quicker, consistent with treatment outcomes observed when managing this category of hypoglycemia.

Skin tone brightened after glucose and described as no longer clammy or excessively cold at the current time in our monitoring. Also clearer in coordination compared to initial response, even when Mr. Smith fell out of the wheelchair and had this sudden change in state.

This is a medical diabetic hypoglycemia event as per standard protocol 3-8 today and will be treated in an outpatient-like rapid recovery approach without further field complications identified where documented.
Administered OBG and D10% solution both as per protocol. There are no other medications required or needed at this time in this setting today.
Mrs. Smith, what is your husband's preferred hospital, as on file with him and you previously?
[COMPANY_NAME] Hospital, please. That's where he goes for all his other related procedures and doctors.
Mr. Smith, we need to move you to the stretcher now for secure transport. May we move you, sir?
Yes, go ahead.
Using harnesses and seat belts for secure transport to a stretcher, keeping in mind his condition as a wheelchair-dependent user due to medical history with bilateral amputation as stated earlier.
Transferred to the stretcher with no disturbances identified, and he's very cooperative right now given current state of alertness after treatment.

Yellow room triage at [COMPANY_NAME]. All interventions completed and full report will be given on arrival. Blood pressure is 128 over 66, pulse is 64, and respirations are 12. Oxygen saturation is currently 89.
Following hypoglycemia treatment using protocol 3-8, patient was handled carefully and securely during transport phase as a wheelchair-bound with amputation and defibrillator.

The patient was initially sluggish and unresponsive to certain questions but has improved significantly after oral glucose and D10% IV, as noted in latest reassessment reports. His baseline blood glucose was 36, which climbed to 45 post-glucose, and now at 86 before we started en route.

IV infiltration noted at hospital at site placed during field care, but no complaints of pain or burning were observed in field reports. This was not obstructive or causative of any issues in our care phase.
Patient has been transported and secured for immediate care at [COMPANY_NAME].
